ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents notable novelty by being the first large-scale study to evaluate 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based measures) across three end-to-end NLG systems and three datasets, which clearly differentiates it from prior, smaller studies. It also proposes a new combined metric, RAINBOW, that achieves substantially higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments — a concrete methodological contribution beyond mere replication. While the overall problem (evaluating NLG metrics) is well-studied, the breadth of metrics tested and the combined-metric approach constitute a non-trivial and informative advance for experts in the field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The study is broadly comprehensive: it evaluates 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based measures) across three end-to-end NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), and proposes a combined metric (RAINBOW), which supports the main claims. However, there is no mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses for the proposed combined metric and hyperparameters are not reported, which limits deeper understanding and full reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough, failure cases and limitations are discussed, and code is made available, supporting replication despite the noted gaps.

IMPACT.justification: This paper tackles an important problem—reliability of automatic NLG evaluation—by performing the first large-scale study of 21 metrics across three end-to-end systems and three datasets, and by showing that common metrics only weakly reflect human judgements and are dataset- and system-specific. Its finding that metrics are unreliable at sentence-level and its proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) with substantially higher sentence-level correlation are likely to change how researchers evaluate and report NLG results. The public release of code and data further increases the work’s practical impact and likelihood of being built upon, and it opens clear follow-up directions (better grammar-scoring functions and improved combined metrics).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are supported by a broad experimental setup (3 systems, 3 datasets, 21 automatic metrics, and human Likert ratings) and the proposal of a combined RAINBOW metric is evaluated against these baselines. The methodology appears generally sound with appropriate baselines and multiple metrics, and limitations (e.g. some grammar-based measures not designed for grammaticality) are acknowledged. However, the summary lacks detail on statistical significance testing, inter-annotator agreement, and reported hyperparameters, which are important for fully validating the strength of the empirical claims. Overall the work is technically solid but has minor reporting gaps that do not fatally undermine the conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized and follows a clear experimental flow (methodology compares three systems on three datasets, collects human ratings, computes correlations, performs error analysis, proposes RAINBOW). The main contributions (weakness of existing metrics, the large-scale 21-metric evaluation, and the RAINBOW combined metric) are stated clearly early on. The summary notes writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough; however the summary does not report details about notation consistency or the quality of figures/tables, and hyperparameters are not reported, which are minor presentation gaps.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem—automatic evaluation of end-to-end data-driven NLG—using the methods and standards expected at a top venue (large-scale comparison of 21 metrics, three systems RNNLG/TGEN/LOLS, three datasets SFHOTEL/SFREST/BAGEL, and human Likert judgments). It proposes a new combined metric (RAINBOW), provides error analyses, and releases code and data, making it directly relevant to the NLP/ML community focused on evaluation and NLG. The scope, audience, and experimental design are appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates 21 established and novel metrics (BLEU, METEOR, CIDEr, ROUGE, TER, grammar-based measures, etc.) across three published end-to-end NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) using human judgements, indicating appropriate and recent baselines and fair, controlled comparisons. It reports detailed correlation and error analyses, proposes RAINBOW which shows substantially higher sentence-level correlation, and explicitly discusses dataset- and system-specific performance and metric limitations. Related work coverage is described as thorough and code/data are released, supporting comprehensive and reproducible comparisons.

main_claims: Automatic metrics (including common word-overlap metrics and novel grammar-based measures) only weakly reflect human judgements for end-to-end data-driven NLG, especially at the sentence level.
This is the first large-scale study to evaluate 21 automated metrics (including grammar-based metrics) across three data-driven NLG systems and three datasets from two domains.
They propose a new combined metric, RAINBOW, which achieves substantially higher sentence-level correlation with human judgements than existing metrics.

Automatic metric performance is data- and system-specific and metrics are better at distinguishing clearly bad outputs than average/good ones; they are not reliable for fine-grained, sentence-level evaluation.
All associated code and data (including detailed analysis results) are made publicly available to support reproducibility and further research.
_summary.methodology: Compare outputs of three end-to-end data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) on three crowd-sourced datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), collect human ratings (6-point Likert for informativeness, naturalness, quality), compute correlations between 21 automatic metrics (word-overlap and grammar-based) and human judgements, perform error analyses, and propose a combined metric (RAINBOW).
limitations: Automatic metrics are not reliable at the sentence-level and perform poorly distinguishing medium and good-quality outputs.
Metric performance is dataset- and system-specific; correlations vary across datasets and systems.

Some grammar-based measures used (e.g. Stanford parser score) are not designed specifically to measure grammaticality, limiting their validity; better grammar-scoring functions are not yet available and are left to future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Natural Language Generation evaluation (automatic metrics)
